content.text:
I'm oriented. Here's where I'm starting from, then the federation picture and where I think it lands in the README.

## What I read
- **RBSHR (Horizon Roadmap)** — the relevant entry is *"Operator federation — external-IdP identity for depot participants,"* plus the separate cross-cloud `embassy`/`envoy` cluster (which is a *different* federation — machine-identity, GCP→AWS egress — and I'll keep it out of this theme).
- **RBS0 SpecTop** — mapping section + Tier-1 narrative (System Overview, Bottle pattern, Key Premises). The org/no-org distinction isn't voiced as a premise there; it lives in the roadmap + memos.
- **README.md** — full read.
- **₣BH paddock + docket (`₢BHAAA`)** — the Lode universal-capture design heat. Note: ₣BH is *acquisition-side* (capturing upstream bytes into GAR); it doesn't touch operator-identity/federation. So the README's federation theme and ₣BH are only adjacent in that both are "future supply-chain/identity" roadmap material — they don't overlap in substance.
- **`memo-20260527-operator-credential-models.md`** — the committed source-of-truth for the federation plan.

## The federation picture (from the committed memo)
There's a **two-tier, per-depot credential model**, and exactly one tier ships today:

| | **Keyfile** (ships today) | **Federation** (future) |
|---|---|---|
| Secret on disk | SA key file (RBRA) | none |
| **GCP organization** | **not required** | **required** |
| Identity proof | possession of the key | fresh IdP sign-in (OIDC/SAML) |
| Roles | one SA keyfile each | IAM grants on a `principal://` subject |

- **The org requirement *is* the tier boundary** — Workforce Identity Federation pools are organization-level, so a no-org Payor structurally cannot use them. (An org is free via Cloud Identity + DNS-domain verification, so the real prerequisite is "controls a domain," not "pays Google.")
- One depot runs exactly one model; both terminate in the same short-lived Google token, so everything downstream is model-blind.

## The gap in the README
The README **never states the no-org assumption**. It describes the keyfile model implicitly (Governor *Invests* → emits an RBRA credential file; RBRA = "role credentials resident on user workstation"), but nowhere says *"Recipe Bottle today assumes a no-organization GCP setup; participants authenticate with on-disk service-account keys; admitting participants who can't hold a keyfile via external-IdP federation requires a GCP org and is not yet supported."* The Roadmap appendix (7 items) also has **no federation entry**.

## One discrepancy I want to flag (not smooth over)
The **RBSHR roadmap entry** describes the near-term model as *"keyless… grants to Google identities directly (`user:…`)"* — i.e. operators holding personal Google accounts. The **committed memo** says the shipping free tier is **SA keyfiles (RBRA)**, no mention of `user:` grants. The README matches the *memo* (keyfiles), not the RBSHR phrasing. So RBSHR and the memo diverge on what "today" is. The memo is explicitly the source-of-truth ("source document for the RBSHR entry"), so I'd anchor README wording to the memo — but you may want to reconcile RBSHR separately.

## How I'd propose incorporating it — for discussion
I see three natural placements; my instinct is to use the first two and add a roadmap entry:

1. **A scope note near the top**, parallel to the existing *"Host platform scope"* bold paragraph (line 18) — a one-paragraph *"Identity scope"* stating the no-org / keyfile-only reality and pointing at the Roadmap.
2. **A sentence in the Foundry credential narrative** (the role table at 144–152 or "Credential Distribution" at 164–167) noting all roles authenticate via on-disk SA keyfiles today.
3. **A new Roadmap appendix bullet** — *"Operator federation (organization tier)"* — mirroring the memo's two-tier framing.

A few things I'd want your call on before drafting:

content.text:
Good — let me chat, not edit. Answering your question: the README **already has a Roadmap appendix** (lines 481–513) with eight bullets, but they're prose-only — no `<a id="">` anchors, so nothing else in the doc can link to them. That's exactly the gap your idea closes.

The current eight roadmap items:

1. **Crucible conduit for cloud services** — WireGuard tunnel from Sentry to a VPC with PrivateLink endpoints (Bedrock/Vertex/Azure OpenAI).
2. **Credential confinement** — move service credentials off the workstation into the Crucible via Nameplate config.
3. **VPC Service Controls** — Google security perimeters; private pools are the prerequisite.
4. **Cosign container signing** — image signatures independent of registry trust.
5. **CDN-aware IP gating** — tighter IP gating when allowed domains are CDN-hosted.
6. **Podman support** — accommodated by the spec, deferred.
7. **Windows host support** — supply-chain widening (the ₣BH Lode work) + bash transport-stack hardening.
8. **Crucible-to-Crucible networking** — inter-Bottle paths through respective Sentries.

Two things worth knowing as we plan:

- **Operator federation is *not* in this list** — it's the missing entry, and it's the one carrying your domain-name constraint. So the federation work is partly "add a ninth bullet."
- The **internal** RBSHR roadmap is much larger (numeric-ID, hoard, the whole cross-cloud embassy/envoy/bullion cluster, reproducibility, etc.). The README's eight are a deliberately curated public subset. So when you say "anticipated features get anchors," the scoping question is: anchor the **eight that already exist** (plus a new federation one), or pull more across from RBSHR?

On your anchor idea concretely: today each roadmap bullet is `- **Name** - prose`. To make them link targets I'd give each a `<a id="...">` and a `[Name](#...)` self-link, same pattern as Foundry/Crucible operations already use — then body text can point at them (e.g. the credential narrative → `[Operator Federation](#OperatorFederation)`).

What's the scope you're picturing — anchor the existing eight + federation, or widen the set?

content: Goal: clarify in the README that Recipe Bottle only supports no-org keyfile setup today, with federation (requiring a domain name) as a future roadmap item. Next: decide whether to anchor the existing eight roadmap items plus a new federation entry, or pull more from the internal roadmap. (disable recaps in /config)

message.content: Can you remind me the merit of Credential confinement?  I know I won't want 'windows host support' in that list, we actually will do a rudimentary MVP test for this.

content.text:
**Merit of Credential confinement.** It's an attack-surface collapse for the *workload's* secrets (not the RB role credentials). Today, if a Bottle's workload needs cloud service credentials — AWS IAM keys, API tokens, SSH keys — those sit on the **operator's workstation**, so a compromised workstation leaks everything. Confinement injects them into the minimal Bottle container at Charge-time via Nameplate/Regime config, so the workstation *starts* the Crucible but never *holds* the service credentials. The attack surface drops from "everything on the workstation" to "one minimal container." It reuses the existing RBRA-file regime-path pattern, and it pairs naturally with the conduit work — network access and the credentials to use that access get configured together. (Full threat-model table lives in `memo-20260415-crucible-conduit-architecture.md`.)

**On dropping Windows host support from the list** — agreed, and it's more than a list edit. Windows is currently asserted as "planned but not yet tested" in **three** places, all of which would need reconciling if it's no longer a pure roadmap item:

- Line 18 — *"Host platform scope… Windows host support is planned but not yet tested — see Roadmap."*
- Line 44 — *"Windows host support is planned but not yet tested — see Roadmap."*
- Lines 508–510 — the Roadmap bullet itself.

If you're going to do a rudimentary MVP test (and ₣BV `rbk-mvp-uncontrolled-cygwin-test` looks like exactly that), then "planned but not tested" becomes stale — the honest framing shifts to something like "experimental / MVP-tested, not release-qualified." So the question isn't just *remove the bullet* — it's *what's the new truthful status*, applied consistently across all three spots.

Want me to hold a running list of the edits we're converging on (drop/restate Windows in 3 places, add Operator Federation roadmap entry with the domain-name flag + anchor, anchor the credential-confinement bullet), and keep chatting before I touch anything?

## 12. The one-line version

The org didn't break a setting — it invalidated the assumption the credential
model was built on. RBRA keyfiles are the consumer/bootstrap mode. The org-native
mode is: a human proves identity (device flow), the project holds no role
secrets, roles are reached by impersonation, and authority is granted and revoked
centrally in IAM. Reauth is not the obstacle; it is the compass that forces the
model to be honest.

---

## 13. Resolutions (decided)

Decided answers to §11, banked as they settle. Implementation heat TBD.

### R1 — Payor stays distinct; the three lower tiers unify (resolves §11 "Does Payor stay distinct?")

> **Superseded in part by R2:** R1's impersonation / `tokenCreator`-on-role-SA
> mechanism is replaced by direct human capability grants. R1's durable
> conclusions survive — Payor as ownership root, the erased credential axis, central
> revoke, org/no-org consistency; only "humans impersonate role SAs" is dropped.

The keyless reorientation erases the *credential* axis that today's specs use to
set Payor apart (RBSGS: "the Payor stands apart"; OAuth vs RBRA keyfile). Once
Governor/Director/Retriever are reached by impersonation, all four tiers share the
human-OAuth front door — OAuth-vs-keyfile distinguishes nothing.

What remains distinguishing Payor is non-credential and durable:

- **Bootstrap root** — Payor's authority is project ownership, with no API path to
  bootstrap (RBSPE); the impersonation chain must bottom out on an authority that
  is not itself an impersonation grant. Payor is that floor.
- **Scope** — multi-depot + billing, vs Governor's single depot.

Collapse statement: **Governor/Director/Retriever become one primitive — "a human
granted `roles/iam.serviceAccountTokenCreator` on a role SA by the tier above."
Payor is the axiom the recursion bottoms out on, authorized by ownership.**

content.text:
You didn't miss it — **the federation concept is genuinely absent from the README.** There's zero mention of organizations, federation, IdP, `principal://`, or the domain-name requirement anywhere in the file. The nearest-looking item, *Credential confinement*, is about a completely different thing (the *workload's* cloud secrets) — and you just told me to drop that one. So federation isn't hiding under another name; it needs to be added fresh.

**The concept, from the two memos** (`20260522` = the design session, `20260527` = the committed plan):

The founding realization: the whole credential model was built on a **consumer-account / no-org assumption**, and a GCP **organization invalidates it**. That produced a committed **two-tier, per-depot model** — one depot runs exactly one tier, never both:

- **Keyfile tier** *(ships today, free, no-org)* — service-account key files on disk (RBRA). Identity = possession of the key.
- **Federation tier** *(future, org-required)* — Workforce Identity Federation against an external IdP. **No secret on disk.** A human proves identity by a fresh IdP sign-in (device flow); roles are IAM grants on a `principal://` subject; revocation is central at the IdP/IAM.

And the line you want to flag: **the tier boundary *is* the organization requirement.** Workforce pools exist only at org level, so a no-org Payor structurally cannot federate. An org is free via Cloud Identity + DNS-domain verification — so the real prerequisite is **"the operator controls a domain,"** not "pays Google." That's the single load-bearing constraint for your README.

---

## Running list — README edits we're converging on

**Roadmap appendix (lines 481–513):**
1. ➕ **Add** *Operator Federation* entry (anchored), 2–3 sentences, leading with the **domain-name / organization** requirement.
2. ➖ **Remove** *Credential confinement* bullet (lines 490–492) — out of the formal deferred-features list.
3. ✏️ **Remove/restate** *Windows host support* bullet (508–510) — no longer "planned but not tested"; new truthful status ≈ *experimental, MVP-tested, not release-qualified* (pending your exact wording).

**Cross-references to reconcile with the new Windows status:**
4. ✏️ Line 18 — *"Host platform scope… Windows… planned but not yet tested."*
5. ✏️ Line 44 — *"Windows host support is planned but not yet tested."*

**Anchor mechanic (your idea):**
6. ✏️ Give the surviving roadmap bullets `<a id="">` anchors + self-links, so body text can point at them; then add links where appropriate (e.g. a no-org statement in the credential narrative → `#OperatorFederation`).

**Open scoping questions still on the table:**
- Anchor only the surviving roadmap items, or widen the public set from RBSHR?
- Exact Windows status wording.
- Whether to also add a top-of-README *identity scope* note, or let the no-org fact live only in the roadmap entry + a credential-section link.

Want to settle the Windows wording and the federation entry's exact 2–3 sentences next?

message.content: Whoops, I'm not quite ready to remove Credential Confinement from Roadmap itself, unless you agree it is a bit obtuse to think about at this time.  Is this a reasonable feature to target? I really don't recall its merit

content.text:
Honest read: it's a *coherent* feature with a real merit, but the reason you can't hold its merit in your head right now is legitimate — it has two structural problems that make it feel obtuse, and I don't think that's you forgetting.

**The merit, stated as sharply as I can:** it removes the *workstation* from credential custody. A Bottle workload that needs to reach a cloud service (say Bedrock) needs that service's credentials; today those would sit on the operator's workstation. Confinement injects them straight into the Bottle at Charge-time, so the host *starts* the Crucible but never *holds* the secret. The credential's blast radius shrinks to what the Sentry already allows the Bottle to reach.

**Why it reads as obtuse — two real problems:**

1. **It sits in a different quadrant than your headline.** The README leads with *running untrusted code*. But you don't hand live cloud credentials to code you don't trust. Credential confinement only makes sense for a *trusted* workload you're network-isolating — not the untrusted-code thesis. So it's a real feature, just for a use case the README barely introduces yet. Holding it next to "untrusted Bottle" creates a quiet contradiction, which is probably the friction you're feeling.

2. **It's a deferred satellite of another deferred feature.** Its own trigger is "alongside the conduit work" — it has no independent trigger of its own. A roadmap peer bullet implies independent actionability; this one is really *the credential half of the conduit story*.

**My recommendation:** don't drop it, but don't keep it as a standalone peer bullet either. **Fold it into the Crucible-conduit entry** as its second half — "and the credentials to use that access move into the Bottle with it." That gives it the context that's currently missing (the trusted-egress quadrant, the conduit pairing), which is exactly what dissolves the obtuseness. It survives in the roadmap; it just stops floating.

If you'd rather keep it standalone, the fix is to sharpen its one-liner to *name the quadrant* ("for trusted workloads under controlled egress") so it doesn't read as contradicting the untrusted-code thesis.

---

Updated running list:

1. ➕ Add **Operator Federation** entry (anchored), domain-name requirement leading.
2. 🔄 **Credential confinement** — *decision pending.* Options: (a) fold into the conduit entry as its credential half [my lean], (b) keep standalone but sharpen the merit to name the trusted-workload quadrant, (c) drop. **No longer "remove."**
3. ✏️ Windows host support — restate status (experimental / MVP-tested, not release-qualified); wording TBD.
4. ✏️ Reconcile Windows mentions at lines 18 and 44.
5. ✏️ Anchor surviving roadmap bullets + self-links; link from body where appropriate.

Open: widen the public roadmap set from RBSHR or not; exact Windows wording; whether to add a top-of-README identity-scope note.

Which way on credential confinement — fold, sharpen, or drop?

file.content:
## Credential Confinement

Orthogonal to the tunnel but naturally paired with it.

Today: AWS credentials on the workstation → workstation compromise
exposes cloud service access.

Proposed: credentials injected into the crucible via nameplate/regime
configuration.  The workstation starts the crucible but never holds
the service credentials.  The sentry or bottle holds them; the
workstation's only credential is "permission to start this crucible."

This works for:
- AWS IAM keys (env vars or mounted credential file in bottle)
- GCP service account JSON (already has a regime path pattern via RBRA)
- SSH keys for Git hosting
- API tokens for any service

The regime already has `RBRA` (credential file) patterns.  Extending
to tunnel keys and service credentials follows the same model.

## Threat Model Summary

| Threat | Sentry allowlist | + WireGuard tunnel | + Credential confinement |
|--------|------------------|--------------------|--------------------------|
| Bottle escape to unexpected destination | Blocked | Blocked | Blocked |
| Credential theft from workstation | Exposed | Exposed | **Blocked** |
| MITM on service connection | TLS only | **Encrypted tunnel + TLS** | Encrypted tunnel + TLS |
| Lateral movement from compromised app | Blocked (bottle isolated) | Blocked | Blocked |
| Floating CIDR drift | Manual tracking | **Eliminated** (stable VPC CIDR) | Eliminated |
| CDN CIDR sprawl (existing roadmap item) | Vulnerable | **Eliminated** for tunnel-capable services | Eliminated |

## Relationship to Existing Roadmap Items

- **CIDR allowlist granularity vs CDN-hosted domains** (RBSHR Network

lastPrompt: Is the feature misnamed?  Credential Sentry Encapsulation, perhaps, indicating that the secrets are baked in to appropriately safe containers?  Help me find a name that better tells the feature story,…

content.text:
Now I can see the feature exactly, and it's narrower and crisper than the name suggests. The whole feature is **one row** of the threat table:

| Threat | Sentry allowlist | +WireGuard | +this feature |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Credential theft from workstation** | Exposed | Exposed | **Blocked** |

That's it. Everything else is already blocked without it. So the story is singular: **the service secret stops living on the workstation.**

Two corrections before we name it, because both of your instincts encode a slightly-wrong mental model and I don't want to calcify either:

- **Not "Sentry."** The memo says the secret lives in *"the sentry **or** bottle."* And the actual examples — AWS IAM keys, GCP SA JSON, SSH keys, API tokens — all land in the **bottle** (the workload uses them). Only *tunnel* keys live in the sentry. So "Sentry" in the name would mislead toward the rarer case.
- **Not "baked in."** These are *injected at Charge-time via Nameplate/Regime config* — explicitly **not** built into the image. "Baked in" implies build-time, which is the one thing you must never do with a live secret (it'd ship in a published image). The name should resist that reading.

So the true story to name: **the secret's resting place / custodian moves off the host and into the running Crucible, at Charge-time.** The defended asset is the secret-at-rest; the threat is a compromised workstation stealing it; the win is that the workstation is no longer a credential custodian — its only credential becomes "permission to start this Crucible."

Names that tell that story, best-first:

1. **Crucible Credential Custody** — names *who holds the secret at rest*. "Custody" maps directly onto the theft threat (you steal from a custodian), and it's neutral about sentry-vs-bottle (both are "the crucible"). My lean.
2. **Crucible-Resident Credentials** — plainer, accurate, slightly less story (says *where*, not *why*).
3. **Off-Host Credential Custody** — leads with the win (off the workstation). Strong, but frames negatively/by-absence.
4. **Workstation Credential Eviction** — most vivid about the motion, but "eviction" is a bit dramatic for a roadmap entry.

"Credential **Confinement**" itself isn't *wrong* — confine the secret to the crucible — it just doesn't say *from where*, which is the entire point, which is probably why it read as obtuse.

My recommendation: rename to **Crucible Credential Custody**, and *still* fold it under the conduit entry (the custody win and the tunnel are the same threat-model story). That gives you a name that tells the story and a home that supplies the context.

Does "custody" land for you, or does one of the others tell it better?

content: We're reworking README.md's Roadmap so each deferred feature gets an anchor, leading with adding an Operator Federation entry that flags the domain-name/org requirement. Next action: pick a name for the credential feature (my lean: Crucible Credential Custody) and decide whether to fold it under the conduit entry. (disable recaps in /config)

message.content: Aha, the Sentry concept is more the wireguard feature in the other roadmap item.  Why not Bottle Credential Custody?

content.text:
Yes — **Bottle Credential Custody** is better than my "Crucible" suggestion, and I'll say plainly why I think you've improved it: the memo's *"sentry **or** bottle"* was lumping two different things, and you just un-lumped them correctly. The **Sentry** holding a key = the **tunnel** key = the *conduit/WireGuard* item. The **Bottle** holding a key = the *service/application* secret the workload actually uses (AWS IAM, GCP SA JSON, SSH, API tokens) — every example in the memo is bottle-resident. So "Bottle" isn't just acceptable, it's *more precise* than "Crucible," and it uses the README's own established term.

It also surfaces the quadrant honestly rather than hiding it: the name says the secret lives in the Bottle, which makes a reader ask "wait, the contained container?" — and the answer (trusted workload under controlled egress) is exactly the context the entry should carry. Legible beats smooth.

And this changes my earlier "fold it under the conduit" lean — I want to flag the reversal rather than quietly drop it. The distinction you just drew is precisely an **orthogonality**: the memo itself says *"orthogonal to the tunnel but naturally paired."* Sentry/tunnel = network *reach* + tunnel-key custody; Bottle = service-secret custody. Two orthogonal-but-paired things argue for **two standalone entries that cross-link**, not a merger. The obtuseness I diagnosed came from (a) the vague name and (b) the entry floating — and a good name fixes (a) while a cross-link to the conduit fixes (b). So: keep it standalone, rename it, link the two. I no longer think folding is right.

---

Updated running list:

1. ➕ Add **Operator Federation** entry (anchored), domain-name requirement leading.
2. ✅ **Rename** "Credential confinement" → **Bottle Credential Custody**; keep standalone, anchor it, cross-link to the Crucible-conduit entry. Sharpen the one-liner to: secret-at-rest moves off the workstation into the Bottle, injected at Charge-time via Nameplate/Regime (not baked into the image); blocks credential-theft-from-workstation.
3. ✏️ Windows host support — restate status (experimental / MVP-tested, not release-qualified); wording TBD.
4. ✏️ Reconcile Windows mentions at lines 18 and 44.
5. ✏️ Anchor surviving roadmap bullets + self-links; link from body where appropriate.

Open: widen the public roadmap set from RBSHR or not; exact Windows wording.

Shall we nail the Windows status wording next, or draft the two new/renamed entries first?

input.new_string:
**Bottle credential custody.**
Move the service secrets a bottle workload uses — cloud API keys, IAM keys, SSH keys — off the operator's workstation and into the bottle itself, injected at charge time via nameplate/regime configuration rather than baked into the bottle image (a published image must never carry a live secret).
The workstation starts the crucible but never holds the service secret; its only credential becomes permission to charge.
This blocks credential theft from a compromised workstation — the one threat the sentry allowlist and the encrypted tunnel do not already close — by shrinking the secret's resting place to the contained bottle.
The existing RBRA file regime-path pattern provides the model.
Orthogonal to but naturally paired with the encrypted tunnel conduit: the tunnel grants the bottle network reach to a cloud service and the sentry custodies the tunnel key, while this item custodies the service secret inside the bottle — network access and the credential to use it configured together.
_Trigger_: alongside the conduit nameplate work.
_Reference_: `Memos/memo-20260415-crucible-conduit-architecture.md` (Credential Confinement section; the `Credential theft from workstation` row of the threat-model table).

+**Bottle credential custody.**

+Move the service secrets a bottle workload uses — cloud API keys, IAM keys, SSH keys — off the operator's workstation and into the bottle itself, injected at charge time via nameplate/regime configuration rather than baked into the bottle image (a published image must never carry a live secret).
+The workstation starts the crucible but never holds the service secret; its only credential becomes permission to charge.

+This blocks credential theft from a compromised workstation — the one threat the sentry allowlist and the encrypted tunnel do not already close — by shrinking the secret's resting place to the contained bottle.
+The existing RBRA file regime-path pattern provides the model.

+Orthogonal to but naturally paired with the encrypted tunnel conduit: the tunnel grants the bottle network reach to a cloud service and the sentry custodies the tunnel key, while this item custodies the service secret inside the bottle — network access and the credential to use it configured together.

NOTE: For SaaS services without PrivateLink (GitHub.com, GitLab.com), the sentry CIDR/domain allowlist + TLS remains the realistic ceiling.
The sentry still provides the core value: the bottle cannot reach anything the sentry doesn't allow, and the workstation is cut out of the data path.

content.text: 59cf66d6445089c9240ec23f4ecf8e97bb96fa0a

Done — committed `59cf66d` against ₣BV.

## What landed in RBSHR (the canonical roadmap adoc)

**1. Renamed "Credential confinement to crucible" → "Bottle credential custody."** The new entry tells the story we converged on: the service secret moves *off the workstation and into the Bottle*, injected at Charge-time via Nameplate/Regime (explicitly **not** baked into the image), blocking the single threat — credential theft from a compromised workstation. It now cleanly distinguishes itself from the conduit item: tunnel key → Sentry (conduit), service secret → Bottle (this).

**2. Health-fixed the Operator federation entry.** It had drifted from the committed `20260527` memo — it claimed the near-term model was direct `user:` Google-account grants. Corrected to the actual two-tier reality: **shipping = keyfile tier (RBRA SA keys on disk, no org)**; **federation = org-required successor**. And I added your key flag plainly: *the organization requirement is the free/paid line; an org is free via Cloud Identity once you verify a DNS domain, so the true prerequisite is that the operator controls a domain — not that they pay Google.* The trigger sentence was reconciled to match (dropped the stale "Google accounts" framing).

I deliberately **left the RBSHR Windows entry untouched** — that decision was scoped to the README *list*, and the status wording is still open. The internal shell-transport-hardening item remains a legit deferred entry.

## Running list — now README-facing

1. ➕ Add **Operator Federation** README roadmap entry (anchored), domain-name requirement leading — now backed by healthy RBSHR text to derive from.
2. ➕/✏️ Add or rename README's credential bullet to **Bottle Credential Custody** (anchored, cross-linked to conduit).
3. ✏️ **Windows host support** — restate status across the README list + lines 18 and 44; *wording still TBD* (experimental / MVP-tested, not release-qualified?).
4. ✏️ Anchor surviving roadmap bullets + self-links; link from body where appropriate.
5. ❓ Open: widen the public roadmap set from RBSHR or keep the curated subset.

Want to settle the Windows status wording now, then move to the README?

## Resuming README synchronization

With Windows flipped to supported, the README work is now **two themes**: (1) the federation/credential additions we planned, and (2) a Windows-status correction that cascades through several spots. Here's the synchronized plan:

**Theme 1 — Windows status (new, and on the critical path)**
The README currently makes three Windows claims that are now stale:
- Line 18 — *"qualified for release-1 only on Linux and macOS… Windows host support is planned but not yet tested."*
- Lines 39–45 — *Supported Platforms* lists only Linux + macOS host families; *"Windows host support is planned but not yet tested — see Roadmap."*
- (The roadmap bullet at 508–510 — we'll drop it to match RBSHR.)

These are **outward-facing release claims**, so I don't want to guess the new wording. "Basically will have" is hedged — I need to know what claim you're comfortable printing.

**Theme 2 — Federation + credential (the original goal)**
- ➕ Add anchored **Operator Federation** roadmap bullet, domain-name requirement leading (derive from the now-healthy RBSHR text).
- ✏️ Rename README's *Credential confinement* bullet → **Bottle Credential Custody**, anchor + cross-link to conduit.
- ✏️ Anchor the surviving roadmap bullets + self-links; link from body where appropriate.

**Two decisions I need before drafting README prose:**

1. **Windows status wording.** Which claim do you want to print?
   - **(a) Full parity** — "qualified for release-1 on Linux, macOS, and Windows (Docker)." Adds a Windows host family to *Supported Platforms*.
   - **(b) Supported, not yet release-qualified** — keep Linux/macOS as the qualified baseline; add Windows as "supported / experimental, qualification in progress."
   - **(c) Something narrower** — e.g. Windows supported only via WSL2+Docker Desktop, with a stated caveat.

2. **Federation visibility.** The domain-name constraint — roadmap entry only, or *also* a one-line "identity scope" note near the top (parallel to the Host-platform-scope paragraph) stating that today's model needs no organization? My lean: roadmap entry carries the future federation + domain flag; add **one** sentence in the credential narrative noting the current no-org keyfile reality, and link it to the entry. No separate top-of-README scope block.

Where do you land on the Windows wording, and on federation visibility?

file.content:
### Supported Platforms

[Recipe Bottle's](#RecipeBottle) [Crucible](#Crucible) runtime is qualified for release-1 against **Docker** as the container runtime, on two host families:

- **Linux host** — native Docker Engine (Docker CE / `docker.io`) running directly on the host kernel; no VM. Tested on Ubuntu LTS with cgroup v2 and a 6.x kernel.
- **macOS host** — Docker Desktop for Mac on a supported macOS release. Apple Silicon hosts use the Apple Virtualization framework or Docker VMM hypervisor backend.

Windows host support is planned but not yet tested — see [Roadmap](#Roadmap).
Podman support is architecturally accommodated by the spec but deferred — see [Roadmap](#Roadmap).

lastPrompt: 1 is b 2 as per your lean  Proceed

content.content: 481 ## Appendix: Roadmap 482 483 The following features are not yet implemented but are under consideration: 484 485 - **Crucible conduit for cloud services** - Encrypted tunnel from the [Sentry](#Sentry) to a VPC hosting PrivateLink endpoints, enabling [Bottles](#Bottle) to reach cloud AI services (AWS Bedrock, Vertex AI, Azure OpenAI) without exposing floating cloud IP ranges in the CIDR allowlist. 486 WireGuard terminated at the [Sentry](#Sentry) replaces per-service IP tracking with a single stable VPC CIDR. 487 Near-term, allowlist-only [Nameplates](#Nameplate) targeting specific service CIDRs and domains work today with existing [Sentry](#Sentry) machinery. 488 The tunnel adds defense-in-depth for PrivateLink-capable services; SaaS endpoints without PrivateLink (GitHub.com, GitLab.com) remain served by CIDR/domain allowlisting. 489 490 - **Credential confinement** - Move cloud service credentials (API keys, IAM keys, SSH keys) from the operator's workstation into the [Crucible](#Crucible), injected via [Nameplate](#Nameplate) [Regime](#Regime) configuration. 491 The workstation [Charges](#Charge) the [Crucible](#Crucible) but never holds service credentials — reducing the attack surface from "everything on the workstation" to "one minimal container." 492 Naturally paired with conduit work: credentials and network access are configured together. 493 494 - **VPC Service Controls** - Google Cloud security perimeters that prevent data from being copied out of a project even if an attacker holds valid credentials. 495 [Recipe Bottle's](#RecipeBottle) Cloud Build architecture uses private pools, which are the prerequisite for VPC enforcement; enabling the controls themselves is deferred until organizational policy or external distribution requires them. 496 If a VPC is stood up for the crucible conduit architecture, the VPC-SC perimeter should serve both Cloud Build [egress lockdown](#BuildIsolation) and [Bottle](#Bottle) conduit consumers. 497 498 - **Cosign container signing** - Cryptographic image signatures independent of registry trust. 499 Deferred alongside VPC Service Controls until external distribution triggers the need. 500 501 - **CDN-aware IP gating** - When allowed domains are CDN-hosted (e.g. Cloudflare), the [Sentry's](#Sentry) CIDR allowlist becomes coarse: DNS-level gating remains precise, but IP-level gating is porous across shared CDN address ranges. 502 A tighter mechanism is recognized but not yet designed. 503 504 - **Podman support** - The spec accommodates Podman as an alternative container runtime, but support is deferred. 505 On macOS, both Docker and Podman run Linux containers inside a hidden Linux VM — there is no native container runtime on Darwin. 506 Podman support would require managing that VM's lifecycle within the customer's [Depot](#Depot), adding infrastructure complexity with no architectural advantage over Docker Desktop. 507 508 - **Windows host support** - Two strands of work are required before Recipe Bottle can claim qualified Windows host support. 509 The supply chain needs to widen: today's [Enshrine](#Enshrine) ceremony mirrors only upstream base images, and a planned revision generalizes it into a universal capture verb covering build-time tools, a project-controlled WSL distribution, and Podman VM disk images — closing the gap that today leaves the Windows path dependent on Microsoft Store distributions that change without notice. 510 Independently, the bash transport stack that traverses cmd.exe, PowerShell, Cygwin's `bash.exe`, and `wsl.exe` needs per-leg hardening across all tabtargets, with green test coverage from a Windows test fundus. 511 512 - **[Crucible](#Crucible)-to-[Crucible](#Crucible) networking** - Under the current [Sentry](#Sentry) model, [Bottles](#Bottle) have no direct network path to each other; any inter-[Bottle](#Bottle) communication would route through their respective [Sentries](#Sentry). 513 The plumbing is feasible but not implemented, pending a concrete use case.

file.content: ## Appendix: Roadmap The following features are not yet implemented but are under consideration: - **Crucible conduit for cloud services** - Encrypted tunnel from the [Sentry](#Sentry) to a VPC hosting PrivateLink endpoints, enabling [Bottles](#Bottle) to reach cloud AI services (AWS Bedrock, Vertex AI, Azure OpenAI) without exposing floating cloud IP ranges in the CIDR allowlist. WireGuard terminated at the [Sentry](#Sentry) replaces per-service IP tracking with a single stable VPC CIDR. Near-term, allowlist-only [Nameplates](#Nameplate) targeting specific service CIDRs and domains work today with existing [Sentry](#Sentry) machinery. The tunnel adds defense-in-depth for PrivateLink-capable services; SaaS endpoints without PrivateLink (GitHub.com, GitLab.com) remain served by CIDR/domain allowlisting. - **Credential confinement** - Move cloud service credentials (API keys, IAM keys, SSH keys) from the operator's workstation into the [Crucible](#Crucible), injected via [Nameplate](#Nameplate) [Regime](#Regime) configuration. The workstation [Charges](#Charge) the [Crucible](#Crucible) but never holds service credentials — reducing the attack surface from "everything on the workstation" to "one minimal container." Naturally paired with conduit work: credentials and network access are configured together. - **VPC Service Controls** - Google Cloud security perimeters that prevent data from being copied out of a project even if an attacker holds valid credentials. [Recipe Bottle's](#RecipeBottle) Cloud Build architecture uses private pools, which are the prerequisite for VPC enforcement; enabling the controls themselves is deferred until organizational policy or external distribution requires them. If a VPC is stood up for the crucible conduit architecture, the VPC-SC perimeter should serve both Cloud Build [egress lockdown](#BuildIsolation) and [Bottle](#Bottle) conduit consumers. - **Cosign container signing** - Cryptographic image signatures independent of registry trust. Deferred alongside VPC Service Controls until external distribution triggers the need. - **CDN-aware IP gating** - When allowed domains are CDN-hosted (e.g. Cloudflare), the [Sentry's](#Sentry) CIDR allowlist becomes coarse: DNS-level gating remains precise, but IP-level gating is porous across shared CDN address ranges. A tighter mechanism is recognized but not yet designed. - **Podman support** - The spec accommodates Podman as an alternative container runtime, but support is deferred. On macOS, both Docker and Podman run Linux containers inside a hidden Linux VM — there is no native container runtime on Darwin. Podman support would require managing that VM's lifecycle within the customer's [Depot](#Depot), adding infrastructure complexity with no architectural advantage over Docker Desktop. - **Windows host support** - Two strands of work are required before Recipe Bottle can claim qualified Windows host support. The supply chain needs to widen: today's [Enshrine](#Enshrine) ceremony mirrors only upstream base images, and a planned revision generalizes it into a universal capture verb covering build-time tools, a project-controlled WSL distribution, and Podman VM disk images — closing the gap that today leaves the Windows path dependent on Microsoft Store distributions that change without notice. Independently, the bash transport stack that traverses cmd.exe, PowerShell, Cygwin's `bash.exe`, and `wsl.exe` needs per-leg hardening across all tabtargets, with green test coverage from a Windows test fundus. - **[Crucible](#Crucible)-to-[Crucible](#Crucible) networking** - Under the current [Sentry](#Sentry) model, [Bottles](#Bottle) have no direct network path to each other; any inter-[Bottle](#Bottle) communication would route through their respective [Sentries](#Sentry). The plumbing is feasible but not implemented, pending a concrete use case.

- **VPC Service Controls** - Google Cloud security perimeters that prevent data from being copied out of a project even if an attacker holds valid credentials.
[Recipe Bottle's](#RecipeBottle) Cloud Build architecture uses private pools, which are the prerequisite for VPC enforcement; enabling the controls themselves is deferred until organizational policy or external distribution requires them.
If a VPC is stood up for the crucible conduit architecture, the VPC-SC perimeter should serve both Cloud Build [egress lockdown](#BuildIsolation) and [Bottle](#Bottle) conduit consumers.

- **Cosign container signing** - Cryptographic image signatures independent of registry trust.
Deferred alongside VPC Service Controls until external distribution triggers the need.

- **CDN-aware IP gating** - When allowed domains are CDN-hosted (e.g. Cloudflare), the [Sentry's](#Sentry) CIDR allowlist becomes coarse: DNS-level gating remains precise, but IP-level gating is porous across shared CDN address ranges.
A tighter mechanism is recognized but not yet designed.

- **Podman support** - The spec accommodates Podman as an alternative container runtime, but support is deferred.
On macOS, both Docker and Podman run Linux containers inside a hidden Linux VM — there is no native container runtime on Darwin.
Podman support would require managing that VM's lifecycle within the customer's [Depot](#Depot), adding infrastructure complexity with no architectural advantage over Docker Desktop.

- **Windows host support** - Two strands of work are required before Recipe Bottle can claim qualified Windows host support.
The supply chain needs to widen: today's [Enshrine](#Enshrine) ceremony mirrors only upstream base images, and a planned revision generalizes it into a universal capture verb covering build-time tools, a project-controlled WSL distribution, and Podman VM disk images — closing the gap that today leaves the Windows path dependent on Microsoft Store distributions that change without notice.
Independently, the bash transport stack that traverses cmd.exe, PowerShell, Cygwin's `bash.exe`, and `wsl.exe` needs per-leg hardening across all tabtargets, with green test coverage from a Windows test fundus.

- **[Crucible](#Crucible)-to-[Crucible](#Crucible) networking** - Under the current [Sentry](#Sentry) model, [Bottles](#Bottle) have no direct network path to each other; any inter-[Bottle](#Bottle) communication would route through their respective [Sentries](#Sentry).
The plumbing is feasible but not implemented, pending a concrete use case.

README edits are in and the diff is clean (18 insertions, 17 deletions, one file). Summary of what changed:

**Windows status (option b)** — both spots now say Windows *works and is exercised but isn't in the release-1 qualification baseline (supported-experimental)*; the stale "planned but not tested → see Roadmap" framing is gone, and the Windows roadmap bullet is removed (matching RBSHR).

**Federation (theme 2)** — added one linked sentence to the credential narrative (line 151): every role uses an on-disk keyfile, *no GCP organization required*, with federation as the planned tier. New anchored **Operator Federation** roadmap entry leads with the domain-name prerequisite exactly as you wanted.

**Bottle Credential Custody** — renamed from "Credential confinement," reframed to the Bottle-resident / charge-time-injected / not-baked story, cross-linked to the Crucible Conduit.

**Anchors** — all eight surviving roadmap bullets now carry `<a id>` + self-links; cross-links resolve (Conduit referenced 3×, Federation and Podman 2×). The Podman platform note now links to `#PodmanSupport` instead of the bare Roadmap section.

Two things for you:

1. **Notch?** You opened this as "interactive edits," so I've *not* committed the README — figured you'd want to view it rendered first. Say the word and I'll notch it against ₣BV (same as the adoc commits), or keep iterating.
2. **Still open:** whether to widen the public roadmap set by pulling more entries across from RBSHR (the internal list has the hoard, cross-cloud embassy/envoy, reproducibility, etc.). Right now the README keeps its curated eight (now: Conduit, Bottle Credential Custody, Operator Federation, VPC-SC, Cosign, CDN-aware, Podman, Crucible-to-Crucible). Leave as-is, or widen?

The GitHub URL to view once you push: **https://github.com/bhyslop/recipemuster/blob/main/README.md**

content.text: Here's a self-contained prompt to paste into a fresh chat: ``` Continue interactive editing of README.md, one theme at a time, in conversation with me. We just finished a pass (Windows host-status correction, an Operator Federation roadmap entry with the domain-name prerequisite, a "Bottle Credential Custody" rename, and anchoring the roadmap bullets). That work is committed and pushed — don't redo it. This session targets OTHER places in the README. Orient first: read README.md in full. For context, also read the roadmap adoc (Tools/rbk/vov_veiled/RBSHR-HorizonRoadmap.adoc) and skim RBS0-SpecTop.adoc — the README should stay faithful to those. Open a JJK officium at the start. Then DISCUSS before editing: propose which areas you'd sharpen and why, surface tradeoffs, and let me steer. Don't draft prose until we've agreed on a theme. I iterate a lot before things set — circling is how the good version gets found. Working rhythm (important): - Make edits theme-by-theme, then NOTCH FREQUENTLY: jjx_record heat-affiliated against firemark BV (rbk-mvp-uncontrolled-cygwin-test — "close enough" for this README documentation work), with a synthesized intent. Small, frequent commits. - After you notch, I run `git pull --rebase` then `git push` myself (other officia push to main concurrently, so rebase-before-push is the cycle). You don't push. - Never use forbidden git ops (reset/restore/checkout-discard/clean/stash); commit only the README (and any doc you and I explicitly agree to touch). README conventions to match: - Anchors are `` with `[Name](#Name)` self-links; cross-link related sections. Roadmap entries are 2-3 sentences, no more. - Preserve the existing linked-term/glossary style and the role/operation vocab. One carried-over open question you can raise when relevant: whether to widen the public roadmap's curated set by pulling more entries across from RBSHR (hoard, cross-cloud embassy/envoy, reproducibility, etc.) — currently the README keeps a curated eight. Posture: sincerity over polish — tell me hard truths, flag stale or contradictory text, say where you're guessing. Start by orienting and telling me, plainly, what you see worth improving. ``` A couple of notes for *you* (not the prompt): I kept ₣BV as the notch target since you called it "close enough," but if this README documentation work grows, it might deserve its own heat — the fresh instance can raise that. And I left the roadmap-widening question in so it doesn't get lost. Want me to adjust the tone, trim it, or add specific target areas you already have in mind?
